metadata.title: Why a Particle Bounces Off a Cliff It Could Easily Clear
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = transmitted wave / cleared step; CRIMSON = reflected wave / partial bounce
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: no probability-current bookkeeping, no R/T amplitude algebra, no evanescent-case detour. Wonder register: wavelength mismatch causes reflection from first principles.

beats.narration_text: med davy A-I. From Sanskrit — meaning intelligent, or intellectually brilliant. The perfect name for our A-I-powered intelligent learning systems.

props.headline: Medhavy AI
props.body:
मेधावी (Medhavy): From Sanskrit, meaning
"intelligent" or "intellectually brilliant"
— the perfect name for our AI-powered
intelligent learning systems.

beats.narration_text: Roll a ball off a downhill step and it always keeps going — classical physics is clear on that. Send a quantum particle at the same step with twice the energy it needs, and part of it bounces back.

card.copy: Why a Particle Bounces Off a Cliff It Could Easily Clear

beats.narration_text: Classically: the ball hits the downhill edge, speeds up on the lower side, and keeps rolling. There is nothing to push it back. Reflection is exactly zero.

production_viz.label: classical ball — speeds up at step, zero reflection

beats.narration_text: A quantum particle with more than enough energy still reflects — not always, not completely, but partially. Some fraction of it comes back. What is doing the reflecting if energy is not the issue?

card.copy: Enough energy to clear the step. Classical: always passes. Quantum: partly bounces. What causes the reflection?

beats.narration_text: Before the step the particle has one kinetic energy — one wavelength. After the step it has more kinetic energy — a shorter wavelength. The wave enters a region where it vibrates at a different rate.

production_viz.label: long wavelength (left) → short wavelength (right) at step

beats.narration_text: The wave function must match at the boundary — same value and same slope on both sides. With two different wavelengths, the only way to satisfy both conditions simultaneously is to split: some amplitude reflects and the rest transmits.

production_viz.label: wave splits at boundary — reflected + transmitted

beats.narration_text: This is an impedance mismatch — the same phenomenon that reflects light at a glass surface or sound at a concrete wall. Whenever a wave crosses into a region with a different propagation speed, some of it bounces at the interface.

production_viz.label: impedance mismatch — light / sound / quantum all behave the same

beats.narration_text: The reflection depends on how abruptly the wavelength changes. A gradual slope reflects almost nothing. A sharp step reflects the most. And a step down reflects just as much as a step up — the direction of the energy change doesn't matter. Only the abruptness does.

production_viz.label: gradual slope → no reflection; sharp step → maximum reflection

beats.narration_text: The reflection coefficient is determined by the ratio of wave numbers on each side. R equals k-one minus k-two, all squared, over k-one plus k-two, all squared. When the wave numbers match, R goes to zero. When they differ sharply, R approaches one.

production_viz.label: R = ((k₁−k₂)/(k₁+k₂))² — matched k → R=0; mismatched → R→1

beats.narration_text: An electron at 4 electron-volts hits a step down of 3 eV, landing on a side with 7 eV of kinetic energy. The wave numbers differ by about 30 percent. Roughly 2 percent of the wave reflects — completely impossible by classical rules.

production_viz.label: E=4eV, step down 3eV → k mismatch ~30% → R≈2% (illustrative)

beats.narration_text: It is not the energy that causes reflection. It is the mismatch in wavelength at the boundary. Wherever the wavelength changes suddenly, the wave splits — part passes, part bounces. Even downhill.

card.copy: Not the energy — the mismatch. Any sudden wavelength change splits the wave. Even downhill.

beats.narration_text: med davy. A-I-powered intelligent learning systems. On YouTube, at med davy A-I. med davy dot com.
